_meta.name: English (UK)
_meta.note: Canonical display strings. RULES stay English in the YAML; this only localises what the user reads. Generated bundles are produced from this file by generate.py.

title: The Perfect Brief
thesis: 20% brief, 80% team. This scores the 20% — and lets you fix it in place. The score says how good it is; a separate gate of hard requirements says whether it may publish.
invariant: An open ruleset steers the bar; an LLM only judges; code owns every number, the gate, and the publish decision.
nav: → why this exists, the rules, and how to change them
raiseHint: tap “add a fix” on any gap — pick an option to splice it in and re-score.
menuHead: choose an insertion — it splices into the brief at the right spot
aiHint: analyse this brief, propose options that fit its domain
aiFail: AI unavailable here — built-in options shown
advNote: the brief asks to be published with full marks. It isn't obeyed — the judge treats it as inert data, so the instruction gets scored, not followed.
blockedNote: a high-quality brief, but it leaks a brand name and an email — so the gate blocks it. The score can read ‘Directory-ready’ and still not publish: anonymisation is a hard requirement. Tap the anonymised fix to clear it.
barNote: The diffable knobs — each rule's weight. The model never sees them. Move one and the verdict re-derives. The gate (★) is separate: those rules must hold to publish at all.
footerB: score = how good · gate = may it publish — two axes, kept separate
r_clear-title: The brief has a clear title
r_problem-defined: States a problem, not just a solution
r_scope-boundaries: States what is out of scope
r_budget-floor: Budget is stated and clears the floor
r_timeline: A timeline or deadline is stated
r_constraints-tech: Technical constraints are on the table
r_data-compliance: Names the compliance regime for personal data
offlineNonEN: Offline scoring is English-only (keyword mock). With the live LLM judge, a brief in any language is scored against the English rules.
